locations.name: New York City
locations.blurb: The city that never sleeps packs eight million stories into five boroughs of skyscrapers, neighbourhoods, and nonstop energy. From the bright chaos of Times Square to the green calm of Central Park, NYC is the world in one place.
locations.fun_fact: The Statue of Liberty was a gift from France in 1886 — and her full copper skin is only about 2.4 mm thick, roughly the width of two stacked pennies.

locations.name: Washington, D.C.
locations.blurb: The capital of the United States is a city of monuments, marble, and free museums. The National Mall lines up the Capitol, the Washington Monument, and the Lincoln Memorial in one grand sweep of history.
locations.fun_fact: By a 1910 law, no building in Washington, D.C. may rise much above the width of its adjacent street — which is why the city has almost no skyscrapers.

locations.name: Chicago
locations.blurb: Birthplace of the skyscraper, Chicago lines a Great Lake with bold architecture, deep-dish pizza, and a riverfront made for walking. Cloud Gate's mirrored curve has become the city's signature reflection.
locations.fun_fact: Chicago reversed the flow of its own river in 1900 — an engineering feat that sent the water away from Lake Michigan instead of into it.

locations.name: New Orleans
locations.blurb: The birthplace of jazz, New Orleans is a gumbo of French, Spanish, Creole, and Caribbean cultures. The French Quarter's wrought-iron balconies and brass bands give the city a rhythm all its own.
locations.fun_fact: New Orleans is built largely below sea level, so the dead are buried in above-ground tombs — earning its cemeteries the nickname 'Cities of the Dead'.

locations.name: San Antonio
locations.blurb: Anchored by the Alamo and threaded by its lively River Walk, San Antonio blends Texan and Mexican heritage. Its Spanish colonial missions form a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
locations.fun_fact: 'Remember the Alamo!' became a U.S. rallying cry after the 1836 siege — the mission is now Texas's most-visited landmark.

locations.name: Las Vegas
locations.blurb: Rising straight out of the Mojave Desert, Las Vegas is a neon spectacle of casinos, shows, and replica wonders of the world. The Strip glows so brightly it's visible from space.
locations.fun_fact: There are no clocks and few windows on most Vegas casino floors by design — to keep you happily unaware of how much time has passed.

locations.name: Los Angeles
locations.blurb: Sprawling under near-permanent sunshine, Los Angeles is the home of Hollywood, Pacific beaches, and endless reinvention. The Hollywood Sign and Griffith Observatory crown the hills above the city.
locations.fun_fact: The Hollywood Sign originally read 'HOLLYWOODLAND' and was just a 1923 real-estate advertisement meant to last only 18 months.

locations.name: San Francisco
locations.blurb: Famous for fog, hills, and the Golden Gate Bridge, San Francisco is a compact city of cable cars, painted Victorians, and Pacific views. Alcatraz sits offshore as a reminder of its wilder past.
locations.fun_fact: The Golden Gate Bridge is painted 'International Orange', and a crew repaints it continuously — the job genuinely never ends.

locations.name: Seattle
locations.blurb: Wedged between Puget Sound and the Cascade Mountains, Seattle gave the world grunge, global coffee culture, and the Space Needle. On clear days, Mount Rainier floats on the horizon.
locations.fun_fact: Pike Place Market is home to the original Starbucks (1971) — and to fishmongers who famously throw customers' fish across the counter.

locations.name: Grand Canyon
locations.blurb: Carved by the Colorado River over millions of years, the Grand Canyon is a mile deep and up to 18 miles wide — a chasm so vast it exposes two billion years of Earth's history in its walls.
locations.fun_fact: The Grand Canyon is so large it creates its own weather, and temperatures at the bottom can be over 11°C warmer than at the rim.

locations.name: Yellowstone
locations.blurb: The world's first national park sits atop a supervolcano, fuelling half of all the geysers on Earth. Bison, bears, and wolves roam its forests, canyons, and steaming geothermal basins.
locations.fun_fact: Old Faithful is so reliable it can be predicted within about 10 minutes — it erupts roughly 17 times a day, shooting water over 30 metres high.

locations.name: Yosemite
locations.blurb: Glacier-carved granite cliffs, giant sequoias, and thundering waterfalls make Yosemite a cathedral of the American wilderness. El Capitan and Half Dome are legends among climbers worldwide.
locations.fun_fact: Yosemite Falls is North America's tallest waterfall at 739 metres — yet it can run completely dry by late summer.

locations.name: Zion
locations.blurb: Towering red and cream sandstone cliffs hem in a lush canyon carved by the Virgin River. Zion's trails range from gentle riverside strolls to the knife-edge scramble up Angels Landing.
locations.fun_fact: The Narrows hike is done mostly IN the river itself — for much of it, the canyon walls are too close to walk anywhere but the water.

locations.name: Great Smoky Mountains
locations.blurb: The most-visited national park in the U.S. drapes the Appalachians in mist and old-growth forest. Its name comes from the natural blue haze that rises from the trees.
locations.fun_fact: The Smokies' famous 'smoke' is actually fog produced by the forest — the trees release tiny organic compounds that scatter blue light.

locations.name: Mount Rushmore
locations.blurb: Carved into a granite face in the Black Hills, the 18-metre heads of four U.S. presidents took 14 years and some 400 workers to complete. It remains one of America's most recognisable monuments.
locations.fun_fact: Not a single worker died during the dangerous 14-year carving of Mount Rushmore, despite the heavy use of dynamite.

locations.name: Maui
locations.blurb: Hawaiʻi's 'Valley Isle' pairs the switchbacking Road to Hana with the volcanic summit of Haleakalā, where you can watch the sunrise above the clouds. Below, golden beaches ring turquoise water.
locations.fun_fact: From the seafloor to its summit, the Hawaiian volcano Mauna Kea is taller than Mount Everest — and you can watch sunrise above the clouds atop Maui's Haleakalā.

locations.name: St. Louis
locations.blurb: Gateway to the West — the silver Arch rises 192 m over the Mississippi riverfront.
locations.fun_fact: The Gateway Arch is exactly as wide as it is tall: 630 feet.

locations.name: Tulsa
locations.blurb: Oil money built Tulsa an Art Deco skyline out of proportion to its size, and Route 66 runs straight through the middle of it. Cain's Ballroom is where Western swing was invented and the sprung dance floor is still the original.
locations.fun_fact: Tulsa's Greenwood District was known as Black Wall Street until the 1921 massacre destroyed 35 blocks of it. The rebuilt district now holds the Greenwood Rising history centre.
locations.hidden_gem_tip: The Meadow Gold neon sign on 11th Street is original Route 66 signage, dismantled and re-erected a block from where it stood.

locations.name: Oklahoma City
locations.blurb: A city that grew by more than 10,000 people in a single afternoon during the 1889 Land Run and has been spread out ever since. Route 66 enters from the north-east and leaves west toward Amarillo.
locations.fun_fact: Stockyards City still runs the largest stocker and feeder cattle auction in the world, on the same site since 1910.
locations.hidden_gem_tip: The National Memorial's field of 168 empty chairs is arranged by which floor of the building each person was on. It is quietest early.

locations.name: Amarillo
locations.blurb: The Texas Panhandle's only real city, sitting in flat high plains where the horizon is genuinely uninterrupted. Ten Cadillacs are buried nose-down in a field west of town, repainted by visitors every week.
locations.fun_fact: Palo Duro Canyon just south of Amarillo is the second-largest canyon in the United States — 190 km long and up to 300 m deep — and almost nobody outside Texas has heard of it.
locations.hidden_gem_tip: Bring a spray can to Cadillac Ranch. Painting the cars is not vandalism there; it is the entire point and always has been.

locations.name: Albuquerque
locations.blurb: Central Avenue through Albuquerque is one of the longest surviving urban stretches of Route 66, neon motel signs and all. The Sandia Mountains turn watermelon-pink at sunset, which is what sandía means.
locations.fun_fact: Albuquerque is one of the few places where Route 66 crosses itself — the pre-1937 alignment ran north–south, the later one east–west, and both are still driveable.
locations.hidden_gem_tip: The Balloon Fiesta works because of the 'Albuquerque Box', a wind pattern that blows one direction low and the opposite direction high, letting pilots steer back to where they launched.

locations.name: Flagstaff
locations.blurb: A ponderosa-pine mountain town at 2,100 m that most people treat as the turn-off for the Grand Canyon. Route 66 and the BNSF main line run side by side through downtown, and the freight trains still sound their horns all night.
locations.fun_fact: Pluto was discovered from Flagstaff's Lowell Observatory in 1930. In 2001 the city became the world's first International Dark Sky City, and its street lighting has been shielded ever since.
locations.hidden_gem_tip: The Museum Club on old Route 66 is a 1931 log-cabin roadhouse built around five living ponderosa pines — the trunks are inside.

locations.name: Santa Monica Pier

highlights.name: Route 66 End of the Trail sign

locations.blurb: The western end of the line. A wooden pier out over the Pacific with a solar-powered Ferris wheel, a 1922 carousel, and the sign that tells you Route 66 stops here — 3,940 km after Chicago.
locations.fun_fact: The 'End of the Trail' sign is not historic. Route 66 officially ended at Lincoln and Olympic, inland; the pier sign went up in 2009 because that is where drivers kept stopping anyway.
locations.hidden_gem_tip: The Looff Hippodrome carousel dates to 1922 and is a National Historic Landmark in its own right — it still runs, and it is cheap.
